findings.description: The PDF page 1 has a clear two-panel side-by-side layout (left table: State-Reported Summary Data with FY 2013 Cumulative Oct and Rank; right table: sorted by Change with FY 2012 Cumulative Oct-Sep/2, FY 2013 Cumulative Oct, and Change columns). The HTML converts this into a single-column sequential layout, losing the side-by-side presentation.
findings.evidence: PDF shows two distinct tables side by side separated by a diagonal shading pattern. HTML renders the left table first as a simple single-column table, then the right table below it, also single-column.

findings.description: The diagonal yellow/green gradient shading pattern between the two tables in the PDF is absent in the HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF has a decorative diagonal striped pattern separating the two table panels. HTML has no such graphic element.

findings.description: The right-side table in the PDF has colored row highlighting (green/yellow gradient backgrounds and a '<' marker for Wyoming) that conveys visual emphasis. The HTML table lacks these row color indicators and the '<' marker.
findings.evidence: PDF right table shows rows with green-to-yellow gradient backgrounds and a '<' symbol next to WYOMING. HTML table has no colored rows and no '<' marker.

findings.description: The left table in the PDF uses colored/highlighted row backgrounds (green gradient) for states, which is absent in the HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF left table rows have green-shaded backgrounds. HTML left table has plain white/no background rows.

findings.description: The bottom '0.00' value that appears at the bottom-right of the PDF page is present in the HTML but appears separated from its context.
findings.evidence: PDF shows '0.00' at bottom right of the page. HTML shows '0.00' at the bottom of the second table section.

findings.description: Reading order differs. In the PDF, a reader would see both tables simultaneously side-by-side, with the left table showing rank-ordered states and the right table showing change-ordered states. In the HTML, the left table content appears first (all 53 rows), then the right table content follows sequentially.
findings.evidence: PDF: side-by-side tables allow parallel comparison. HTML: sequential presentation forces linear reading of left table then right table.

findings.description: Nested <main> elements in the HTML. The document has two <main> tags, one nested inside the other, which is invalid HTML structure.
findings.evidence: The HTML has <body><main>...<main>...</main></main></body> with two nested main elements.

findings.description: The 'Report Run Date: March 14, 2013' appears twice in the HTML, once for each section. In the PDF, both dates appear as headers for the two side-by-side tables on the same page, so this is an acceptable representation of the source layout.
findings.evidence: PDF shows 'Report Run Date: March 14, 2013' twice (once for each table panel). HTML reproduces this faithfully.
